title: Snow cornice

infobox.value: or simply **cornice** (from the Italian **cornice** meaning "ledge") is an overhanging edge of [snow](/wiki/Snow "Snow") on a ridge or the crest of a [mountain](/wiki/Mountain "Mountain") and along the sides of [gullies](/wiki/Gully "Gully").

Wilhelm Welzenbach: *Untersuchungen über die Stratigraphie der Schneeablagerung und die Mechanik der Schneebewegengen nebst Schlussfolgerung auf die Methoden der Verbauung*, Karlsruhe 1930, Wissenschaftl. Veröffentlichungen d. D. u. Ö. Alpenvereins. Nr 9

Seattle: Mountaineers Books. p. 546. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9781594854088](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9781594854088 "Special:BookSources/9781594854088").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Overhanging edge of snow

This article is about the snow formation. For the architectural use, see [cornice](/wiki/Cornice "Cornice").

[![Danger zone on a cornice](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Snow_cornice.jpg/500px-Snow_cornice.jpg)](/wiki/File:Snow_cornice.jpg "Danger zone on a cornice")

Danger zone on a cornice

A **snow cornice** or simply **cornice** (from the Italian **cornice** meaning "ledge") is an overhanging edge of [snow](/wiki/Snow "Snow") on a ridge or the crest of a [mountain](/wiki/Mountain "Mountain") and along the sides of [gullies](/wiki/Gully "Gully").

## Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Snow_cornice&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Formation")]

A snow cornice forms by wind blowing [snow](/wiki/Snow "Snow") over sharp terrain breaks (e.g. the crest of the mountain) where it attaches and builds out horizontally. This build-up is most common on the steeper and [leeward](/wiki/Leeward "Leeward") sides of mountains.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Cornices are extremely dangerous and travelling above or below them should be avoided.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

When a cornice *collapses*, it breaks in from the cornice to the top of the peak; even being on the snow on top of rock exposes the alpinist to hazard in this situation. The best practice in [mountaineering](/wiki/Mountaineering "Mountaineering") is to stay far enough back from the edge so as not to be able to see the drop, as an approximate metric of exposure.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In [avalanche](/wiki/Avalanche "Avalanche") safety, cornices are a high avalanche danger as they often break and trigger larger avalanches that permeate several snow layers. Cornices are particularly vulnerable to collapse during periods of high [solar irradiance](/wiki/Solar_irradiance "Solar irradiance").

## Gallery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Snow_cornice&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Gallery")]

Snow cornices

* [![A cornice of snow about to fall. Cracks in the snow are visible in area (1). Area (3) fell soon after this picture was taken, leaving area (2) as the new edge.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Mount_Windsor_Cornice2.jpg/250px-Mount_Windsor_Cornice2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mount_Windsor_Cornice2.jpg "A cornice of snow about to fall. Cracks in the snow are visible in area (1). Area (3) fell soon after this picture was taken, leaving area (2) as the new edge.")

  A cornice of snow about to fall. Cracks in the snow are visible in area (1). Area (3) fell soon after this picture was taken, leaving area (2) as the new edge.
* [![A piece of snow cornice which has fallen off a ridge in early spring](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/FallenCornace.jpg/250px-FallenCornace.jpg)](/wiki/File:FallenCornace.jpg "A piece of snow cornice which has fallen off a ridge in early spring")

  A piece of snow cornice which has fallen off a ridge in early spring
* [![The summit of San Jacinto Peak covered by a cornice formed by wind-blown snow](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Snow_cornice_on_San_Jacinto_Peak.jpg/250px-Snow_cornice_on_San_Jacinto_Peak.jpg)](/wiki/File:Snow_cornice_on_San_Jacinto_Peak.jpg "The summit of San Jacinto Peak covered by a cornice formed by wind-blown snow")

  The summit of [San Jacinto Peak](/wiki/San_Jacinto_Peak "San Jacinto Peak") covered by a cornice formed by wind-blown snow
* [![Massive snow cornices line the summit ridge of Siula Grande, and are a characteristic feature of this peak.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Siula_Grande_West_Face.jpg/250px-Siula_Grande_West_Face.jpg)](/wiki/File:Siula_Grande_West_Face.jpg "Massive snow cornices line the summit ridge of Siula Grande, and are a characteristic feature of this peak.")

  Massive snow cornices line the summit ridge of [Siula Grande](/wiki/Siula_Grande "Siula Grande"), and are a characteristic feature of this peak.
* [![Alternating cornices on the Rochefort-Crest in the Mont Blanc area in the Alps—always overhanging above the steeper face of the crest](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Rochefortgrat1965.jpg/250px-Rochefortgrat1965.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rochefortgrat1965.jpg "Alternating cornices on the Rochefort-Crest in the Mont Blanc area in the Alps—always overhanging above the steeper face of the crest")

  Alternating cornices on the Rochefort-Crest in the [Mont Blanc](/wiki/Mont_Blanc "Mont Blanc") area in the Alps—always overhanging above the steeper face of the crest
* [![Giant cornice on top of the Chopicalqui at 6,345 m (20,817 ft) in the Andes in Peru](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Chopicalqui.jpg/250px-Chopicalqui.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chopicalqui.jpg "Giant cornice on top of the Chopicalqui at 6,345 m (20,817 ft) in the Andes in Peru")

  Giant cornice on top of the [Chopicalqui](/wiki/Chopicalqui "Chopicalqui") at 6,345 m (20,817 ft) in the [Andes](/wiki/Andes "Andes") in [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru")
